coredata.dc:title: Design and development of molten metal nanomaterials using sonochemistry for multiple applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Vijay Bhooshan
coredata.dc:description: Molten metals have prospective applications as soft fluids with unique physical and chemical properties, yet materials based on them are still in their infancy and have great potential. Ultrasonic irradiation of molten metals in liquid media induces acoustic cavitation and dispersion of the liquid metal into micrometric and nanometric spheres. This review focuses on the synthesis of mmetallic materials via sonochemistry from molten metals with low melting point (< 420 ᴼC): Ga, Hg, In, Sn, Bi, Pb, and Zn, which can be melted in organic or inorganic media or water and of aqueous solutions of metallic ions to form two immiscible liquid phases. Organic molecule entrapment, polymer solubilization, chiral imprinting, and catalyst incorporation within metals or metallic particles were recently developed to provide novel hybrid nanomaterials for several applications including catalysis, fuel cells, and biomass-to-biofuel conversion. In all cases where molten metal was sonicated in an organic solvent, in addition to a solid precipitant, an interesting supernatant was obtained that contained metal-doped carbon dots (M@C-dots). Some of these M@C-dots were found to exhibit highly effective antimicrobial activity, promote neuronal tissue growth, or have utility in lithium-ion rechargeable batteries. The economic feasibility and commercial scalability of molten metal sonochemistry attract fundamental interest in the reaction mechanisms, as the versatility and controllability of the structure and material properties invite exploration of various applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molten metals have prospective applications as soft fluids with unique physical and chemical properties, yet materials based on them are still in their infancy and have great potential. Ultrasonic irradiation of molten metals in liquid media induces acoustic cavitation and dispersion of the liquid metal into micrometric and nanometric spheres. This review focuses on the synthesis of mmetallic materials via sonochemistry from molten metals with low melting point (< 420 ᴼC): Ga, Hg, In, Sn, Bi, Pb, and Zn, which can be melted in organic or inorganic media or water and of aqueous solutions of metallic ions to form two immiscible liquid phases. Organic molecule entrapment, polymer solubilization, chiral imprinting, and catalyst incorporation within metals or metallic particles were recently developed to provide novel hybrid nanomaterials for several applications including catalysis, fuel cells, and biomass-to-biofuel conversion. In all cases where molten metal was sonicated in an organic solvent, in addition to a solid precipitant, an interesting supernatant was obtained that contained metal-doped carbon dots (M@C-dots). Some of these M@C-dots were found to exhibit highly effective antimicrobial activity, promote neuronal tissue growth, or have utility in lithium-ion rechargeable batteries. The economic feasibility and commercial scalability of molten metal sonochemistry attract fundamental interest in the reaction mechanisms, as the versatility and controllability of the structure and material properties invite exploration of various applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In the microtechnology and nanotechnology arenas, nanoparticles (NPs) of metallic materials attract great interest due to their physical and biological properties, which have unlocked many new pathways. Metallic NPs can be synthesized by several methods and modified to allow binding to numerous organic molecules including polymers, ligands, antibodies, proteins, DNA and drugs [1]. Metallic nanomaterials are known for their optical [2], magnetic [3] and electronic [4] properties, chemical inertness [5] and numerous biomedical applications [6], and may be functionalized with different chemical entities [1]. Hybrid metal NPs attract increasing attention, reflected in the rising number of publications over the last decade [7–9]. However, these hybrid metallic particles also show the synergistic effects in medical therapies, medical devices, surfaces, and manufacturing of drugs.

Low melting point metals (Hg, Ga, In, Sn, Bi, Cd, Pb, and Zn) and metal-based alloys/intermetallic materials are expected to become future generation materials for several applications [10]. The conventional molten metal definition refers to melting points near or below ∼420 °C [11–13], however Hg and Ga are considered as liquid metals at room temperature or slightly above it. Hg metal has the lowest melting point of all metals (-38.8 °C) but is associated with several health and environmental hazards [14,15], while Ga shows melting point of 29.8 °C and is considered to have low toxicity, therefore it is more suitable for many applications [12,16,17]. The melting points [18] and surface tensions [19] of several elements are presented in Table 1 . There are several reasons for choosing 420 °C as an upper limit: many modern industrial processes regulate below this temperature, in particular polymer melt processing [12,20], opening up the possibility of new processes for nanocomposites; common laboratory instruments like as ovens, furnace, and hot plates operate in this range; and low melting point metals may be co-processed with other materials such as water or organic/inorganic liquids that are routinely processed at increased temperature. In addition to the low melting point, it is important to understand the fascinating properties presented by molten metals in the bulk to nano and at their interfaces [10,12,20].

In 2010, Reich et al. reported a two-step method for obtaining micro/nano spheres of lower melting points metals, by sonication in silicone oil, which has high flash-point and boiling point [16,22]. In this method, a granule of the metal or alloy is immersed in a quartz-based beaker containing silicone oil, and other solvent system is heated using a Bunsen burner. The melting of the metal which produces two immiscible liquids was followed by ultrasonic irradiation causes dispersion of the molten metal into solid micro- and nano-particles. The method was applied to pure metaalic Ga, In, Sn, Bi, Pb, Zn and Hg and two eutectic binary alloys/intermetallic compound of AuGe and Au-Si [16]. All suspensions in silicone oil were obtained, and the particles were separated and analysed. Of particular interest are mercury and gallium, which can undergo cavitation even in warm water. Eutectic AuSi alloy was found to form particles in two size ranges: micrometric spheres with a large variety of surface distribution patterns of the two elements and nanometric spheres composed of pure crystalline gold [16,22].

Metal particles can be prepared by numerous synthesis methods including laser ablation, combustion, pyrolysis, hydrothermal method, precipitation, and sonochemistry [23–25]. Each method has its own pros and cons, e.g. chemical synthesis involves contaminants, which induce cytotoxicity and thus limiting biological application. Development of innovative techniques remains a challenge. Sonochemical methods offer a highly localized green synthesis for several nanomaterials [26,27]. Such methods facilitated various chemical reactions and enabled biological materials featuring unique morphology and intrinsic physico-chemical properties [28].

The typical process described in this review involves the melting of a metal granule under a high boiling point solvent, followed by the sonication of the mixture of the molten metal and the solvent. The tip of the sonicator is placed in the solvent. At the end of the sonication a solid precipitate containing metallic particles is obtained. The metal is precipitated despite of the temperature being well above the melting point. The supernatant is comprised of metal particles incorporated in carbon dots (M@C-dots). Thus, this reaction leads to a general formation of Metal doped C-dots, which is applicable to the eight low melting point metals. Another way to obtain Metal doped C-dots was introduced by Zhang et al. He described a simple synthesis of C-dots and metal (Au, Pt, Pd)‑carbon particles by utilizing the vitamin B2 based salt riboflavin 5-monophosphate sodium salt hydrate as both reducing agent and carbon source [29]. A novel method for the synthesis of Metal doped C-dots was performed by a prolonged photo-irradiation (up to ∼3 h) that was developed in order to dope primarily zero valent metals in C-dots [30]. This long irradiation is inferior to the short (3–10 min) sonochemical method, which yields zero valent metals doped in C-dots as well as solid metal particles that precipitate even at temperatures well beyond the melting point of the metal [31–34].

As explained above, ultrasonication of molten metals produces two different phases: a solid precipitate and a supernatant [35–37]. Our research on the synthesis and applications of molten metal micro-/nanosphere started nearly decade ago. Here, we focus on sonochemical preparation of solid metal/alloy/intermetallic NP-based materials, which precipitate after 3–10 min despite the high temperature. The solid precipitates were tested in a wide range of studies including chiral separation [38], entrapment of organic molecule [39], catalysis [40,41], neuron cell growth [42], fuel cells [43] and superconductivity [44]. Over a dozen research papers were published on properties and applications of solid precipitates of molten metal NPs, yet we are not aware of any review that focuses on formation of metal microparticles and NPs from molten metals. Here, we review sonochemical synthesis of metal micro/nanospheres and alloy/intermetallic nanospheres, entrapment of organic molecules, the effect of sonichemical parameters on physical-chemical properties, and a wide range of several applications. Scheme 1 presents an overview of molten metal sonochemistry including fabrication of nanomaterials, properties, mechanisms, and future potential applications.
It is worth mentioning that important results were obtained on the M@C-dots which are obtained simultaneously with the solid precipitate and found in the supernatant above the it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sonochemistry of molten metals
Sonochemical synthesis of metal micro/nano particles
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, new sonochemical synthesis routes were developed for the formation of micro/nanospheres of various low-melting materials: pristine metals(Ga, In, Hg, Sn, Bi, Pb, Zn) alloys (InSn, BiIn, AgGa, AuGa, CuGa, GaPt, GaC) which are formed in the solid precipitate and metal-decorated carbon dots(Ga@C-dots, Ga@C-dots@Ga, Sn@C-dots@Sn, Pb@C, Sn@C-dots) found in the supernatant after the sonication. Compounds dissolved in liquid media are entrapped within metal particles upon formation and leach out slowly [43,48]. Molecules were also found to be entrapped/imprinted on the surface of particles, as shown with chiral molecules [41]. Application of the ultrasonic method to mixtures of two molten metals or of a molten metal and ionic solution/s of other metal/s can form alloys or intermetallic compounds [49].

Metal NPs were prepared from molten metals via a two-step process mentioned above [19,22]. The sonication vessel was first heated by direct Bunsen flame until the low melting point metal was melted under the liquid medium (silicone oil, water, dodecane), forming two immiscible liquids. Then the ultrasonic tip was dipped in the medium, and the system was irradiated with ultrasonic energy for 3-10 min, which dispersed the metal phase into microscopic particles, as shown in Fig. 1a. Stable solid metal particles were formed, even though the temperature of the medium was above the melting points of the metals. When sonication was ceased, these spheres precipitated to the bottom of the vessel and did not recombine to form bulk liquid metal. This synthesis method was applied to seven pure molten metals such as Ga, In, Sn, Bi, Pb, Zn, and Hg and to some binary intermetallic/alloys. Several parameters were found to affect the formation of the metal microspheres, including ultrasound frequency, power and amplitude, reaction temperature, and sonication duration. The size range of these microspheres is usually between 10 and 10,000 nm [45–49]. In most of the cases, the particles were synthesized under silicone oil (except for Ga in which water is chosen as the solvent in addition to organic solvents). The effect of the reaction parameters on molten metal sonochemistry is discussed briefly below. Additionally, Lu et al . have developed a sonication of molten metal at a different temperature control system for optimizing the performance of liquid metal (LM) nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 1b [50]. The increased the product yield represented by the significantly increased particle concentration, the diminished overall particle size, the prevention of oxide nanorod formation, and the versatility of production of different alloy nanoparticles. Utilizing the same sonication method, Lu et al. design a brushed polyethylene glycol polymer with multiple phosphonic acid groups to anchor the LM NPs [50]. The NPs produced using these strategies remain stable for at least six months in biological buffers [50]. Consequently, the proposed methods have the potential to unlock the vast potential of LM NPs in biomedical applications. In a recent study, Yamaguchi et al. reported the formation of LM NPs through reversible control of the size of LM NPs under sonication [51]. After reaching its minimum size of 35 nm at 20 °C, the size did not increase even when the temperature was raised to 50°C. The reason for this result can be ascribed to the good stabilization of the Ga NPs by C12SH and the thin oxide layer on the surface (Fig. 1c) [51].

Consequently, the size of the Ga NPs needs to be controlled by tuning their stabilization with C12SH molecules and thin layer of oxide. Furthermore, He et al. have used ultrasonication for low melting point metals (Ga, In, EGaIn) to produce LM NPs in metallic or oxide form [52]. In Fig. 1d, the initial state of the deformation (composition and morphology) is shown along with the final products [52]. Nanoscale spheres composed of low melting point EGa-In materials can be designed into microrod of gallium oxide hydroxide (GaOOH) crystals and NPs of Indium (In) attached to their surfaces under appropriate conditions, as demonstrated previously. According to reports, Ga tends to form an oxide layer of Ga2O3 on particle surfaces due to its negative Gibbs free energy (ΔG = −998.3 kJ mol − 1) [50,53]. In addition, Ga2O3 may react with water to form GaOOH in aqueous solutions [54]. Thus, we suggest that LM NPs will follow the chemical synthesis route as shown in Fig. 1 during the shape and size transformation process in order to test the chemical reaction pathway involved. We have recently also reported the formation of molten Ga NPs via sonochemistry [55]. Additionally, Kourosh et al. have developed a polyaniline nanocomposite with embedded EGaIn NPs. As part of the system design process, EGaIn NPs are functionalized with a molecular level of polymerization species, as well as acidity and mechanical agitation [56]. The system also displays synergistic properties that can support environmental sensing [56]. The same group of researchers (Kourosh et al.) recently reported on the use of Ga-based liquid metals for catalysis and CO2 conversion [57]. According to Kourosh et al., it may be possible to develop a closed cyclic catalytic system by mixing liquid metals of Ga and Ag. This procedure can then convert CO2 into several value-added chemical via a reversible Ga+-Ga cycle [57]. In the research community, metal NPs with low melting points are of great interest due to their variety of applications in various scientific fields. Chemical synthesis techniques allow for the control of several NPs feature including morphology, dispersibility in both type of solvent (aqueous and organic), and surface chemistry that is suitable for a variety of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and chemical characterization of Ga micro/nanospheres
General view of Ga micro/nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: In general, top-down and bottom-up synthetic routes are available to synthesize Ga-based nanoparticles. The former involves the reduction of bulk Ga to nano- or micro-sized particles, while the latter involves the formation of NPs from simple molecules. The top-down method, which involves a series of processes that produce large quantities of micro/nanomaterials, such as sonication and shearing, is the most widely used method for the synthesis of low melting point metallic particles. As part of the top-down method, molten gallium was sonicated in water or organic liquids, such as n-hexane and dodecane. Due to its low melting point (∼29.8 °C), gallium could be melted in normal hot water (∼50 °C) or organic solvents that are not very volatile [58]. Ultrasonic cavitation of such a liquid based system can generate cavitation that involves formation, growth, and energetic collapse of gas bubbles [59,60]. The collapse of liquid bubble can affect the dispersion of molten gallium into nanoscale to microscale spheres, which remain stable after irradiation, even when the temperature of the medium was well above (75 °C) the melting point of pristine gallium metal [58]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Ga particles, formed in water, are shown in Fig. 2a–c. The Fig. 2a displays a cluster of spheres in a wide range of sizes, from ∼10 μm down to ∼40 nm; one particle (marked by an arrow) appears to be partially squashed (Fig. 2b), suggesting that the spheres are hollow. Rectangular crystallites are observed at high magnification (Fig. 2c) with rather uniform appearance in terms of shape and size, about 200 nm long and 20 nm wide [58].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns showed minor diffractions assigned to GaO(OH) in the sample prepared in water [58,61], while no oxide or hydroxide were present in sonication under hexane or dodecane. The observation that the spheres did not re-coalesce at temperatures above the melting point of Ga was described by the formation of either a GaO(OH) crystallite layer (when prepared in water) or a coating of a carbon layer (in organic solvent). An evidence for the hollowness of the spheres was obtained by embedding a sample of Ga particles prepared in water in Epson epoxy and slicing the solid bulk by a microtome [58]. The slices were not “transparent” enough for transmission electron microscopy (TEM), but examination by SEM micrograph explained a clear some hollow Ga microsphere, which are shown in Fig. 2d. This one-step sonochemical fabrication method was applied to other low melting point metals in several liquid media including an aqueous solution of organic molecules and organic/inorganic liquids. Very smooth spheres without any crystalline features on the surface were obtained in hexane and dodecane (Fig. 2e & 2f).

The effect of ultrasonication duration on the quality of the product was tested by reaction methods explained above for times spanning from 30 s to two hours. SEM images of some of the products (Fig. 2h-2l) show that the number of crystallites on the gallium surface increases with increasing sonication time. Many observations are seeming from this series of images: a) after 30 s of sonication, only a few crystallites can be observed on the surface (Fig. 2h), indicating the unification of some spheres to form much larger oval-shaped particles. b) After 2 min of sonication, crystallites can be clearly seen on the surface, and no coalescence can be detected (Fig. 2i). c) After 30 min of sonication, a considerable amount of GaO(OH) crystallites is present, while the relative size of the spheres in considerably smaller (Fig. 2j). d) Two hours of irradiation at high amplitude (> 50%) turns all the metallic gallium into GaO(OH) crystallites [61] as shown in Fig. 2k. The progress of the sonochemical reaction was described on the basis of XRD analysis of products. Fig. 2l presents the XRD pattern of products obtained at various sonication times, describing the slowly change from pristine Ga to GaO(OH) phase after one and two hours [61]. The experimental diffractogram matches the orthorhombic crystal structure for GaO(OH) (PDF 540910). The XRD pattern of Ga sphere formed in organic liquid (Fig. 2g) is assigned to a single crystal phase of metallic gallium. No peaks corresponding to GaO(OH) are present. Underneath some of the diffraction patterns, a broad XRD peak appears between 30–40o, explaing the existence of partly liquid gallium. The full ultrasonic conversion of Ga into GaO(OH) presents a novel route for the synthesis of GaO(OH) NPs, which are a precursor for preparing Ga2O3. This is a facile one-step synthesis which requires only pure water and gallium [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attempted formation of gallium carbide
ce:section.ce:para: It is believed that unlike carbides of Boron and Aluminium (B4C, Al4C3), gallium carbide is not stable and therefore cannot be formed [62]. An attempt to form such a carbide by ultrasonic irradiation of molten gallium under solvents such as decane or dodecane, resulted in dispersion of the molten gallium into micro-/nano- spheres [63]. These spheres were characterized by many analytical tools, including XRD, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), TEM, Raman and FTIR spectroscopy, and found to be composed of GaC complexes [63]. The DSC results reveals that the Ga had possibly reacted with carbon to form a Ga carbon complex or gallium carbide, while the Raman analysis demonstrates formation of carbon on the Ga particles [63]. These GaC complexes was further investigated by the extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) and X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) techniques. EXAFS and XANES both demonstrate that the gallium is found in a more reduced state, mostly in Ga zero state (metallic Ga). This may indicate that gallium exists in a kind of complex with carbon [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Entrapment of organic compounds in molten gallium particles
ce:section.ce:para: Entrapment of organic compounds in metals was investigated by Avnir et al., who introduced a new family of hybrid metal–organic composites [47,64–68]. The content of the organic materials in these hybrid composites was in the range of 0.2 to 10 % w/w, enough to induce unique physical and chemical changes in the properties of the metals. Following this concept, we investigated the entrapment of organic compounds in metallic gallium particles [39]. Four water-soluble organic dyes were selected for proving the concept of entrapment of 1,10-phenathroline, Congo red, crystal violet and Rhodamine 6G (Fig. 3 ). All showing strong absorption in the UV–Visible spectrum, which allow the measurement of their molar concentrations by absorption spectroscopy. All these organic compounds were entrapped in the solid Ga. Crystal violet and Rhodamine 6G have fluorescent properties and thus can measured by fluorescence microscopy techniques too. The experimental procedure was as follows: A granule of gallium was melted in a test tube containing a warm aqueous solution of one of the organic compounds. Irradiation with ultrasonic energy caused dispersion of the liquid gallium into microspheres, which precipitated slowly after sonication was ceased. In the case of 1,10-phenanthroline, the naked eye observed that the intensity of the orange colour of the supernatant has diminished in comparison to the intensity of the solution before sonication. The UV–vis spectra showed a considerable reduction in the absorption intensity of 1,10-phenanthroline (Fig. 4a). No changes in the absorption spectrum were noticed in a control experiment (without gallium), which explained that sonication alone does not make any degradation of phenanthroline molecules in the absenteeism of molten gallium (Fig. 3a). Thus, it was concluded that some of the 1,10-phenanthroline was adsorbed to the gallium microspheres or entrapped within them.

The presence of phenanthroline was demonstrated by placing the solid gallium particles in water and following the leaching of phenanthroline by spectroscopic measurements (Fig. 3b). Through a long series of experiments, it was concluded that any detectable phenanthroline must originate from within the bulk of gallium particles. A similar experiment was conducted with a slightly larger quantity of Ga and phenanthroline for prolonged leaching time, followed by acid dissolution of the gallium particles (Fig. 3c). Quantitative measurements of the phenanthroline concentrations in all the samples, using the absorption band at 265 nm, yielded mass-balance that was in accordance with the initial concentration. This concept was further proved with Congo red, which has a typical UV–visible spectrum that includes three absorption bands at 244, 343 and 495 nm (Fig. 3d–f).

Entrapment in Ga particles of the two fluorescent molecules crystal violet and rhodamine 6G with excitation wavelengths of 526 and 543 nm, respectively was also attempted in the same manner as described above. These dyes were conveniently detected by fluorescence microscopy. The precipitated gallium particles were collected, washed three times with water, and dried in a vacuum. SEM analysis showed spherical Ga particles in both cases with same narrow size distributions as collected for phenanthroline. The UV–Vis spectra of crystal violet show that 24% (wt.) were entrapped in Ga spheres after sonication. The entrapment was confirmed with confocal fluorescent microscope. Phase contrast mode reveals many particles (Fig. 3g), while fluorescence mode reveals red dots originating from crystal violet clusters (Fig. 3h). When these two images are compared, the red dots coincide with the particle locations (Fig. 3i), which indicates that crystal violet is contained within the particle or adsorbs onto its surface. Sonication is also an effective technique for entrapping different materials inside metallic spheres. Molecular molecules are slowly released from the spheres, which may be used for biomedical and catalytic purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stiffening of Ga particles by entrapment of organic dye
ce:section.ce:para: Stiffness and strain hardening of structural materials are highly desired physical properties, especially for high-strength engineered metals and alloy particles [69]. It has been demonstrated that materials with variable stiffness are useful in numerous fields, particularly in soft robotics. However, the stiffness regulation process can only be triggered by external voltage or high temperatures, which restricts its use in many situations, such as underwater. Herein, Kumar et al. designed the organic molecules entrapped gallium particles with 4-5 times higher stiffness with respect to pristine Ga NPs in organic/water. They have measured the increased stiffness of gallium micro/nanoparticles using PeakForce Tapping® technology. The relative stiffness analysis was done with molten Ga particles formed in decane, water, and aqueous solution of the three-organic dye (Congo red, phenanthroline, and crystal violet). Comparative X-ray analysis of the gallium particles formed in decane, water and aqueous solutions of the three organic dye entrapped in Ga particles reveals that particles synthesized in decane are mostly crystalline but have also somewhat amorphous structure, while particles synthesized in pure water include crystallite GaO(OH) formation on the surface [70]. The particles formed in the aqueous solutions demonstrated very similar stiffness values, regardless of the identity of the organic compound [71]. These stiffness values of Ga particles were about five times higher than the particles formed in pure decane [71]. The results indicate that entrapment of a certain number of organic compounds within the metallic gallium spheres increases their mechanical strength via minor changes in their crystalline structure. Recently, Liu et al. synthesized a Ga-eGaIn NPs that can be used to maintain the stiffness of copper wire mesh and improves the mechanical activity of the original mesh [72]. Besides the improved stiffness of Cu mesh, the electrical resistance of the mesh treated with the enhancer was also decreased by 10% when compared with the raw Cu mesh [72]. As the temperature increases, electrical resistance stability varies with the composition of the material, which is primarily affected by the saltation resistance difference during the phase-change process [72]. The liquid metal nanoparticles dispersed in gallium-based LM were used in hydraulic transmission fluids, demonstrating the ability to achieve hydraulic output and actuator with tuneable stiffness based on phase change, and providing experimental support for further application of LM hydraulics [73]. Liang et al. demonstrated the rupture stress of liquid metal nanoparticles and their application in stretchable conductors and dielectrics [74]. Furthermore, Chang et al. have investigated non-traditional approaches to fabricating autonomously stiffening materials [75]. Rather than rely on stress-induced chemical reactions, they utilize the metastability of liquid metal particles undercooling [75]. All of these new concepts have been studied in order to create reconfigurable modular soft robotics for a wide variety of future applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of metallic ions by molten gallium under ultrasonic irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
Sonication of metals with higher melting points (>420 °C) is not possible by our technique because of the limitation on the liquification of the metal. However, due to the phenomenon of melting-point depression for nanometric particles, we planned to start from nanoscale metallic particles, which could be enable their melting together with other metals such as Ga, indium, or bismuth. Alternatively, metallic gallium might be used to form nanoparticles of metals with high reduction potentials (such as gold and silver) [76]. It is possible to melt gallium and irradiate it with ultrasonic energy if gallium is present in excess stoichiometrically after reduction. Practically, it was found that the heterogeneous reduction of solid metallic gallium was very slow, whereas melting the gallium, followed by ultrasonic irradiation, enhanced the reduction rate dramatically. According to the standard reduction potentials, three of the metals that were used (Ag, Au, and Cu) should be spontaneously reduced by metallic Ga, whereas another (Zn) should not. Here are the general standard potentials for these reactions:
(1)

Au, Ag and Cu were indeed formed by fast spontaneous reduction of their ions on the large unoxidized surface of Ga particles. In addition, some intermetallic compounds (AuGa2, Ag2Ga, CuGa2) were formed by reactions of the Ga with the reduced metals [76]. In the reduction of gold ions, Gold, gallium, and the intermetallic phase of AuGa2 were all detected in the XRD analysis [76]. Based on Rietveld's analysis of the XRD data, their relative quantities depend on the initial ratio between Au and Ga (Fig. 4a, b). AuGa2 is one of three known gold‑gallium intermetallic compounds [77–81].

The next example is the synthesis of CuGa2 nanomaterials from an aqueous solution of copper sulphate and molten gallium. XRD and SEM images are shown in Fig. 4c, 4d, respectively. The SEM images explained porous materials that can be made-up of clustered of smaller NPs of CuGa2 and metallic Cu. The same reaction was done with copper acetate aqueous solution, and some unreacted gallium was observed, which had not been dispersed into particles. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of both materials shows two main signals corresponding to Ga and Cu. Figs. 4a, 4b and 4c, 4d show the formation of AuGa2 and CuGa2 intermetallic compounds, respectively. The same method was also applied for making AgGa, ZnGa, PtGa, for the detection of other intermetallic compounds [76]. This sonochemical method opened a new approach for the formation of various intermetallic materials with various applications in physics, catalysis, and biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sonochemical synthesis of LM alloy microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: Metallic mercury has the lowest melting point among all metals. Recently, different phases of HgAg amalgam microspheres were synthesized by the sonochemical reaction of molten Hg with an aqueous silver ions solution [82]. The detailed synthesis procedure of HgAg materials is shown in Fig. 5a. As the reduced Ag+ ions form metallic Ag, they are adsorbed on the surface of micro/nano-sized particles of Hg to form amalgams. In terms of size, the HgAg amalgam microspheres are between 6 and 10 μm in diameter. It has been investigated in detail the thermal and chemical behaviour of AgHg systems [82]. The sonication of different molar ratios of liquid Hg and AgNO3 resulted in the formation of pure crystals of a solid silver amalgam with controlled morphology. A sonochemical reaction can result in a pure phase amalgam of Hg and Ag. On the other hand conducting the same reaction with the same reagents ratio under vigorous stirring (Fig. 5b) did not yield the amalgam [82]. The final product, HgAg amalgam, show various compositions after sonication, which were physically characterized [82]. Fig. 5c shows the compositions of Hg and Ag in weight and atomic percentage. Moreover, the HRSEM images give additional properties regarding the surface of the HgAg microspheres. There are few small NPs on the surface of the microspheres (Fig. 5c). The composition formed with different ratio of Hg:Ag 1.5:1 and 2:1 M can be formed different type of particle. In contrast, the particles on the surface of the microsphere are much larger with a ratio of 2:1 in the Hg:Ag composite (Fig. 5c) [82]. They have a diameter of 1–2 μm, are relatively hexagonal in structure, and appear to be much larger than those observed in the composite. The particles on the surface of the microsphere are <1 μm in size and are irregularly shaped in the Hg:Ag composite whose ratio is 1.5:1 [82]. Typical liquid metal alloys, which have unique physicochemical properties, are emerging as the next generation of functional materials for a wide range of biomedical applications. Recently, Zhu et al . reported the surface engineering of liquid-metal based nanoplatforms by adopting a sonosynthetic top-down approach for the formation of inorganic SiO2 nanoshell stabilized low melting point NPs (Fig. 5d) [83]. This facile sonochemical results in more efficient, good targeted, and near-infrared (NIR)-triggered photothermal cancer treatment in the NIR bio window as shown in Fig. 5d [83]. Liquid metal cores with inorganic silica shells exhibit significantly improved photothermal performance, as evidenced by enhanced NIR absorption. During the sonication process, the low melting point metal was mixed into smaller portions and within a few minutes it has been reduced to micro-and/or nanoparticles, while exposing the pristine metal to localized but extremely strong cavitation effects during subsequent sonication periods, resulting in dispersed nanoparticles, which remained uncoated because of the smooth passivation “skin” on their newly generated surfaces [83]. In addition to sonicating the slurry in alkaline ethanol-water solution for an additional 2 h, a rough silica shell was coated, resulting in a thicker coating of the liquid metal core by an additional 3 nm (Fig. 5e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid mercury (Hg) nanoparticles at room temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) is one of the metallic elements that exist as liquids at room temperature, which has a wide range of applications [10,53]. Moreover, Hg metal is also coming under the molten metal category, producing Hg nanoparticles via molten metal sonochemistry [53]. Harika et al. have recently reported the formation of molten Hg NPs supported on solid carbon [84]. They used graphene, graphite oxide, reduced graphene oxide (RGO), graphite, etc., for depositing the molten Hg in the form of solid Hg via ultrasonication under ambient conditions [84]. They explained that sonicating molten Hg in RGO containing aqueous medium forms stable Hg NPs, as shown in Fig. 6a. A possible mechanism for solid mercury nanoparticles formation consisting of the stages is shown in Fig. 6b. Harika et al. were noticed that some of the table Hg clusters could be dispersed within molten metals solution to create liquid-solid heterogeneous phase [84]. The capacity to dope metallic Hg with vastly reduced graphene oxide opens a new avenue to electronic and hybrid materials for multifunctional applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bismuth-based binary alloys/intermetallic particles
ce:section.ce:para: Alloys are mixtures of two or more metallic solid solutions. Intermetallic compounds are a type of alloy, which has long-range ordering, defined stoichiometry and ordered crystal structure with no clear phase boundary. Alloys/intermetallic particles in the form of microparticle/NPs can shows superior physical and chemical properties over the bulk material [11,85]. Binary and ternary alloy nanoparticles were investigated for the effect of their size on the catalytic properties, on the reactivity and stability of the NPs, as well as for numerous potential applications [85–87].

Alloys and intermetallic particles are formed by sonochemistry of a two or more metallic system. We developed the idea to make a particle of a binary alloy composed of Bi and other low melting metal (Sn, In, Ga or Zn) by sonicating the molten mixture of the two metals under silicon oil. Binary mixtures of various stochiometric ratios of Bi and one of these pristine low melting point metals were melted together in hot silicone oil (above the melting point of the metal) and irradiated with ultrasonication to create alloy NPs. The synthesized materials were analysed using several analytical techniques to study the size, shape, chemical nature, and crystal structure of synthesized NPs. It was observed that Bi can be create metal matrix composites with Sn and Zn, intermetallic compounds with metallic In, and an alloy with Ga. Bi and In formed three intermetallic compounds: BiIn, Bi3In5, and BiIn2. Their proportions depend on the initial chemical composition between the metallic Bi and In: when In is in excess, more BiIn2 is formed [88].

A recent study by Tang et al. has also synthesized BiSn nanoalloys by using a liquid-phase based sonication method and examined their phase separation, surface stability in term of oxidation/reduction, and nucleation [89]. Ultrasonication is used to create catalytic nanoalloys that provide high yields, low costs, and avoid unwanted by-products [90]. The multimetallic nanoalloys known for their multifunctional applications (such as catalysis, energy, biomedical applications, etc.). Therefore, Tang et al. have created binary intermetallic/alloys that explained no intermetallic phase, which simplifies the study and makes it more explanatory (Fig. 7 ) [89]. It has been demonstrated in Fig. 7a that synthesizing bulk BiSn alloys is an effective method for analysing phase separation during solidification at various solidification ratio of BiSn metal. The liquid phase sonication of these bulk liquid metallic system is then conducted at an elevated temperature in order to break down the bulk samples into nanoalloys [89]. Tang et al. also examined the atomic-scale structure and defect formations in the BiSn nanoalloys when compared to their bulk counterparts (Fig. 7d) [89].

The photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants (Fig. 7b) and electrochemical catalytic reduction of CO2 (Fig. 7c) of BiSn nanoalloys of different ratios are then investigated. The article by Tang et al. discusses why the sample with the eutectic composition performs best in the catalytic process. Fig. 7e-7g shows transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of BiSn liquid alloys after sonicating them at elevated temperatures. This fully solidified nanoalloy particle form has spherical truncated shapes after post-processing. These NPs range in size from 10 nm- 100 nm (Fig. 7e-h). Moreover, Fig. 7f, g shows the bright field high resolution TEM (BF-HRTEM) images with grain boundaries in nanoalloys. Fig. 7h shows STEM-EDX maps of Bi and Sn; partial oxidation is illustrated by O. As shown in Fig. 7i and j, the annealed nanoalloys exhibit pseudo-first-order degradation kinetics with regard to the Congo red model dye [89]. Fig. 7j shows that the eutectic sample exhibits the greatest degradation at 500 °C when annealed. As shown in Fig. 7k, comparing the several sample ratios reveals that the heated eutectic alloys have the highest k (rate constant), which reveals the fast dye degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential application of sonochemical derived metal nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of molten metals and metal-based nanomaterials were synthesized, with unique and novel properties. These are excellent materials, which may find diverse applications in different branches of science. In this section, we discuss the application of molten metal hybrid particles and alloys based on other metals (e.g., Ga, In, Sn, Pb or Pt). Intermetallic and metal-doped carbon dots are discussed in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chiral imprinting in molten gallium
ce:section.ce:para: A chiral molecule has two mirror images, which are not superimposable in 3D space. It is well known that chirality plays an important role in many molecular recognition processes, which have a wide range of applications in our everyday lives [17,91]. Various methods were developed to design, and detect enantiomerically active chiral molecules. The use of these techniques is essential for the preparation of pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and food additives. Asymmetric catalysis, chiral separation, and enantioselective detection have all been achieved using chiral metal nanoparticles and nanostructures in recent years [17,91–95].

Some organic compounds, such as phenanthroline, Congo red, crystal violet and rhodamine 6G were entrapped in the gallium particles when the sonochemical process entailed molten Ga under aqueous solutions of these compounds [39]. The entrapment experiments were repeated with chiral molecules. In entrapping the chiral molecules, we checked the prospects of chiral imprinting in metallic gallium particles. The experiment was conducted by sonicating D- or L-tryptophan aqueous solutions above liquid Ga probing whether these molecules imprint chirality in the solid Ga [38]. The investigation of chiral imprinting was analysed by circular dichroism (CD), chiral high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and optical rotatory dispersion (ORD) of the aqueous solution. UV–visible analyses for aqueous solutions before and after sonication with molten gallium are depicted in Fig. 8a for D-tryptophan [38]. No UV–Vis spectral changes were observed in terms of the shape and intensity of D-tryptophan with or without Ga (as a control experiment), and it is also explained that sonication alone does not affect the tryptophan molecule. The experiment was conducted as follows: a long leaching process in which the entrapped enantiomer was removed from the sonicated product. After the leaching of chiral molecules from gallium particles were separated and dried. The chirality results were obtained from an experiment performed with the leached (L-tryptophan was entrapped) Ga particles. After immersion of the particles in a racemic DL-Trp solution kept for 7 days with shaking, a sample of solution, together with samples of the original L-isomer solution (before and after sonication) were taken and analysed by polarimetry, CD and chiral HPLC. Fig. 8b presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of aliquots of the leaching tryptophan solution as a function of time over several days. The same experiments were also conducted for the L/DL-tryptophan. Similar outcomes were obtained in both cases.

Fig. 8c presents four CD (Circular Dichroism) curves: a) the original L-trp aqueous solution, b) the solution after sonication with molten Ga, c) the leaching aqueous solution after 15 days of immersion of the L-imprinted particles, and d) the DL-aqueous solution in which the L-imprinted particles were immersed. CD analysis reveals 12% enantiomeric excess (ee) of L-tryptophan [38]. Finally, the quantification of ee of D and L tryptophan was carried out by chiral HPLC analysis, and ∼ 12.6 % ee was found, supporting the CD and ORD results. This concept of chiral imprinting is represented schematically in Fig. 8d. The experiment was repeated with indium metal and obtained 15% ee, which proves the concept of this surfactant-free sonochemical pathway for chiral imprinting on molten metal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic properties of Ga2O3 nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: During the energy crisis of the 1970s, many countries started to develop biomass as a biofuel source [96]. The catalytic conversion of biomass to biofuel is expected to be a significant source of energy in the near future [96]. Furfural is a versatile biomass-derived platform compound that can be used to synthesize several strategic chemicals, which are currently produced using petroleum [97–100]. Xylose is a readily available and underutilized substrate that can be obtained through the hydrolysis of hemicellulose. It is still a key pending issue in bio-refineries to convert pentose sugars into value-added products.

Kumar et al. worked out a facile efficient sonochemical preparation of Ga2O3 nanorods from molten Ga metal for biomass to biofuel conversion. The combined ultrasonication synthesis and shorted calcination time for catalyst preparation (6 h) compared to other conventional techniques [101,102]. The sonochemical synthesized β-Ga2O3 solid catalyst shows good catalytic activity and selectivity in the conversion furfural from of xylose. This solid β-Ga2O3 nanorod catalyst has a high surface area because at the higher temperatures of their formation, particles are smaller and well separated (Fig. 9a), which increases its catalytic performance and enables it to complete conversion of xylose to furfural at 150 °C in short tie (within 12 h) without any by-products. The furfural product was confirmed by HPLC (Fig. 9b) and 13C NMR (Fig. 9c)[40]. GaO(OH) nanomaterials has also catalyse this reaction, however, an appreciable amount of by-products, including lactic acid, were detected in the product mixture. The overall reaction of xylose to furfural is represented in Fig. 9d. This new facile sonochemical reaction for the synthesis of Ga2O3 NPs from pristine Ga metal in water is a new concept for biomass to biofuel conversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nano Ga with molybdophosphoric acid for levulinic acid production
ce:section.ce:para: Scientific and technological studies established that it is possible to generate a wide variety of bioenergy from biomass residues and waste of plant resources, however, its cost is not competitive with petroleum fuels and other resources of renewable energy [96]. An attempt was made to resolve this issue by developing a unique sonochemical method for the synthesis of a Ga-HPMo catalyst for the conversion of waste biomass to levulinic acid (LA) under different hydrothermal reaction conditions [103]. Simultaneous synthesis of GaHPMo NPs and entrapment of HPMo (polyoxometallate) in Ga NPs (Ga@HPMo) was achieved via sonication of liquid Ga [103]. The amount of entrapped hetropoly acid in the Ga NPs was estimated at around 3 wt%.

The synthesized GaHPMo catalyst was used for conversion of glucose, starch, cellulose and biomass (rice straw) into LA via a hydrothermal reaction process [103]. The reaction products were determined qualitatively by NMR (13C and 1H) and estimated the yield by HPLC. The maximum yield of LA gained from glucose was around 56 wt%, with minor amounts of by-products such as formic acid and lactic. The overall chemical reaction of the synthesis of LA from glucose and biomass is depicted in Fig. 10 . LA and other fine chemicals may be produced using this method at the industrial level using diverse cellulose-containing waste biomass such as agricultural wastes, kitchen wastes, paper mill sludges, and cellulose fines from the papermaking process. The results of this method indicate that the cost of LA production might be as low as $0.07/lb., depending on the scale of the operation. In a similar way, a second catalyst—Ga modified zeolite (Ga@mordenite) was developed for biomass to LA production [41]. A synergistic effect was observed. The Lewis acid sites of Ga3+ were produced in situ, and the Brønsted acid sites of zeolite were found to be very important for the catalytic activity of Ga@mordenite in glucose/biomass to LA the conversion [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: GaPt2 and GaPt3 particles as electrocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Platinum (Pt) is the most active electrode in fuel cells, acting as a catalyst in electrochemical reactions. Research is currently focused on developing catalysts that exhibit enhanced electrocatalytic activity [104–107] and durability [108], such as Pt/C composites or Pt alloys with transition metals. Moreover, the reduced amount of expensive Pt makes them more affordable. Therefore, new hybrid intermetallic of GaPt3 and GaPt2 nanoparticles were developed, and their simultaneous deposition on graphene surface was performed by the sonochemistry of molten Ga and H2PtCl6 aqueous solution with graphene flake [43] in a one-step process. The designed Ga-Pt/G NPs were examined as an electrode material for the MOR (methanol oxidation reaction) and ORR (oxygen reduction reaction). This novel synthesized electrode exhibited better MOR and ORR activity than a commercial Pt/C catalyst [43]. The morphology of synthesized GaPt3 and GaPt2 nanoparticles were analysed by high resolution TEM and its revealed that the particles were embedded in the graphene (Fig. 11a, b). The crystal structure of Ga-Pt/G NPs was analysed by XRD technique and shows five broad signals, which is well matched with the PDF database of two different intermetallic phase of GaPt2 and GaPt3 (Fig. 11c). Electrochemical oxidation of methanol was evaluated using GaPt2 and GaPt3 on graphene electrode by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The CV performance of the Ga-Pt/G catalyst was compared with that of a commercial Pt/C (20% Pt/C). The voltammograms presented in Fig. 11d show 0.440 and 0.510 V (vs. RHE) onset potentials over the Ga-Pt/G and Pt/C catalysts, respectively. Observing a positive shift of 70 mV with Ga-Pt/G indicates a lower energy barrier, which may contribute to faster oxidation rates [109]. After complete oxidation of methanol to CO2 during the forward scan, the remaining unoxidized products were oxidized further during the backward scan, yielding the current density I b [109]. Therefore, these synthesized catalysts (GaPt3 and GaPt2) are highly selective towards complete oxidation of methanol, as could be evaluated from the ratio of the forward to backward current densities (I f/I b) in the cyclic voltammogram [109]. The second electrochemical reaction was evaluated from rotating ring disc electrode (RRDE) measurements performed with a carbon electrode with GaPt3 and GaPt2 catalysts in aqueous solution of 0.5 M HClO4 saturated with O2 at room temperature. Fig. 11e compares the RRDE curves for Ga-Pt/G and Pt/C electrodes at a rotation rate of 1600 rpm, showing half-wave potentials of 0.819 and 0.791 V, respectively. Based on Fig. 11f, RHE was conducted at a scanning rate of 10 mVs-1 in an oxygen-saturated 0.5 M HClO4 solution at several rotational speeds (800-2500 rpm) at a range of potentials ranging from 0.4 to 0.6 V with reasonable linearity, indicating consistent electron transfer for oxygen reduction at different electrode potentials.

The durability of the GaPt3 and GaPt2 catalyst-based electrodes was analysed by performing 5000 repetitive CV between 0.6 and 1.0 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Fig. 11 g, 11 h illustrate the I-V curves of the specific current density vs. potential of Ga-Pt/G and Pt/C before and after the RRDE test. Both electrodes exhibit a negative shift in the E1/2 values after cycling: 36 mV for the Pt/C modified electrode and 22 mV for the Ga-Pt/G electrode (Fig. 11i). The E1/2 negative shift indicates the formation of PtOH and PtO resulting from water oxidation, which results in the dissolution of Pt particles [110]. The new synthesized GaPt/C electrode showed higher activity in MOR and ORR than a commercial Pt/C catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Superconductivity of molten Sn and Pb spheres
ce:section.ce:para: The phenomenon of superconductivity occurs when the electrical resistance suddenly drops to zero at its transition temperature. It has been among the hot research topics for decades. Superconductivity at room temperature is one the interesting scientific problems, which could greatly affect the energy transportation and storage [111,112]. The physicochemical properties of nanomaterials differ from their bulk counterpart. Hence, a number of researchers investigated the superconductive characteristics of nanoscale materials [113]. The major issues are agglomeration and oxidation. A promising approach to overcome these drawbacks is to coat nanomaterials with carbon layer [114]. Shani et al. fabricated tin nanospheres coated with a carbon layer via the sonication of molten metallic tin under silicon oil at 350 °C. The tin nanospheres size ranged from 120 to 1400 nm. In addition, it has been demonstrated that type 1 superconductivity with a critical field (Hc) is comparable to bulk tin based on measurements of the magnetic moment of the prepared product. This has resulted in a reduction of the effective coherence length below the size of the carbon-coated tin nanospheres [44]. In spite of this, room-temperature superconductivity remains a challenge. Among the synthesized samples, Pb nanosphere with thin carbon layer demonstrated enhanced Hc due to the reduction of the effective coherence length below their size [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bimetallic Hg/Pd alloy for catalytic reduction of 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP)
ce:section.ce:para: Metals and their alloy of bi/tri-metallic NPs have considerable attention in different fields, including medicine, optics, electronics, and catalysis, due to their unique physical and chemical properties [115]. Recently, Harika et al. synthesized the Hg/Pd alloys by molten metal-based sonochemistry in an aqueous Palladium (II) nitrate solution with metallic liquid mercury [116]. The chemical and morphological analysis of Hg/Pd alloys was confirmed by XRD, HRTEM., SAED, and EDS methods [116]. To explore the catalytic performance of the synthesized Hg/Pd nanoscale alloys and illustrated the 4-nitrophenol formation mechanism in Fig. 12a and overall (synthesis and catalytic of 4-nitrophenol) reaction are presented in Fig. 12b. In addition to that Harika, et al. have also tried the catalytic reduction of few other nitrophenol derivatives and illustrated in Fig. 12c. The Hg/Pd catalytic activity of the nitrophenols in this study was found in order of 4- amino-3-nitrophenol (4A-3-NP) > 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) > 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) > 2,5- dinitrophenol (2,5- DNP) [115,116]. They observed that the 1:1 ratio of Hg: Pd nanoalloy activity significantly higher than other Pd-based catalytic materials, which shown rate constants are smaller by one order of magnitude. The synthesized Hg/Pd nanoalloy catalytic material also showed more stability with recyclable [116].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low melting point metal nanoparticles for biomedical applications
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, low melting point metals are used in numerous areas of research like as mechanical/material engineering, electronics, and energy storage/conversion. In the last few decades, several physicochemical properties of gallium and gallium-based low-melting-point alloys have been appreciated, including low viscosity, excellent fluidity, high thermal, good electrical conductivity, and better biocompatibility [54,117–119]. So, metals have been considered to be a significant class of biomaterials for a long time [117–119]. The exceptional mechanical strength of metals makes them ideal for use as transplants to replace injured tissues, but their potential application for biocompatible materials are limited. However, low melting point metallic nanoparticles provide greater potential because of their unique properties and have allowed the use of metal-based nanomaterials for a wide variety of biomedical applications [118,119]. These low melting point metallic particles are already being used in biomedical applications and are shown in Table 2 .

As a comprehensive survey of low melting point metals with high operating temperatures can be found elsewhere, this article focuses primarily on low melting point metal nanoparticles that interact with ultrasound at low temperatures (Table 1). Except for the oxide layer, few reports have described the decoration of carbon dots on the surface of LM NPs, there are fewer organic molecules on the surface of LM NPs. Furthermore, LM NPs precipitate rapidly in aqueous solutions without the need for a surfactant (usually a thiol-containing group)[54,119]. As a result of this thiol-LM interaction, LM NPs can be surface functionalized very efficiently [52,128]. Upon ultrasonically dispersing a bulk LM, the multifunctional thiolated ligands are readily attached covalently to LM NP surfaces [120]. In addition, their adverse side effect could be severe, and they will unable to prevent metastasis. In general, conventional treatments result in fluctuations in plasma drug concentrations, which are harmful to the patient. Studies have revealed that intratumoral delivery of drugs by direct injection into a tumour mass is an effective way to deliver extremely high doses of drugs to the target site with minimal toxicity because due to intravenous chemotherapy is directly linked to the severe complications and treatment discontinuation [120].

Researchers have developed low melting metal-based drugs that are functionalized molten metal and drugs. Recently, Lu et al . used thiolated 2-hydroxypropyl-b-cyclodextrin and hyaluronic acid (HA) with thiol group as drug loading carrier and targeted moiety, respectively (Fig. 13a, b, and e) [120]. The components of the doxorubicin-encapsulated design of the transformable solid-liquid metal system for cyclodextrin-44 thiolated with Dox can be seen in Fig. 13b. These combinations of LM NP/Dox-L were used to deliver Dox to nuclei for the treatment of targeted cancer tumours, as shown in Fig. 13c and d [120]. Moreover, In a short period of time, Cy5.5-(LM NP/Dox-L) displayed a high fluorescence in the tumour area than Cy5.5-(LM NP/Dox) without the HA-targeting tissue/organ (Fig. 13f). As the experiment progressed, improved fluorescence intensity of Cy5.5-(LM NP/Dox-L) were detected at the tumour/cancer tissue as relative to the healthy tissues within 48 h of post-injection, explain that the nanospheres are capable of targeting tumours [120]. A significantly higher fluorescence signal was recorded for Cy5.5-(LM NP/Dox-L) at the tumour site than for Cy5.5-(LM NP/Dox) (Fig. 13g). In accordance with the quantitative region-of-interest analysis, the fluorescence intensity of Cy5.5-(LM NP/Dox-L) at the tumour/cancer location was 1.8 times that of Cy5.5-(LM NP/Dox) (Fig. 13h) [120].

Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at the tumour/cancer location was three times greater than that found in the healthy liver/kidney (Fig. 13g, h). LM NP/Dox-L has been further tested in HeLa tumour-bearing xenograft mice for antitumor activity. In contrast to saline as a negative control, different Dox formulations displayed significant tumour-inhibiting effects after successive intravenous administration into HeLa tumour-bearing mice (Fig. 13i, k, l). Inhibition efficacy of both LM NP/Dox and LM NP/Dox-L against HeLa tumour growth was remarkably better than that of pristine Dox solution, which was mainly due to the target tumour ability of these NPs. During the course of treatment with LM NP/Dox-L, there was no substantial change in mice body weights (Fig. 13j) [120]. Following LM NP/Dox-L administration, the histological images stained with haematoxylin and eosin revealed that a massive remission of cancer cells occurred in the tumour tissue (Fig. 13m), and no obvious pathological abnormalities were observed in normal organs, including cardiomyopathy, the most significant toxic effect of Dox cancer treatments [120]. Additionally, the images obtained by in situ TUNEL analysis revealed the most significant level of apoptosis in the tumours collected from mice treated with LM NP/Dox-L (Fig. 13n), indicating that LM NP/Dox-L had a significant impact on tumour inhibition. In contrast to conventional metal particles that cannot undergo dramatic morphological changes, this formulation is transformable and can fuse nanoparticles with each other in mildly acidic endosomal/lysosomal microenvironments. This will promote the Dox release. Upon testing in vivo, this nano-platform demonstrated significantly greater inhibitory efficacy compared with free Dox [120]. It is likely due to the hyaluronic acid moiety functionalization, which is one of the main components of the tumour's extracellular matrix (ECM) and may bind to the over-expressed cyclodextrin-44 receptor on solid tumours [120].

In addition to this, Chechetka and colleagues have reported the development of light-driven liquid metal nano-transformers for biomedical theranostics, as well as several other groups [121]. Recently, Lu et al. were able to trigger mechanical disruption of cellular structures by incorporating EGaIn nanoparticles with photosensitizer Ga quantum dots (GQDs) (Fig. 14 ) [129]. When light is irradiated, the zero-dimensional NPs transform into 1-D nanorods, attended by a increase in both the aspect ratio and volume of the metallic NPs structures [129]. As a result of the NPs change from nanospheres to hollow nanorods, the endosomal membrane can be physically disrupted to promote endocytic escape [117,129,130]. They have encapsulated Dox drug and rhodamine-labeled BSA dye into LM NPs through π–π stacking bond and metal-thiol interactions, respectively [129]. The incresed endosomal escape of encapsulation of drug was confirmed by fluorescence quantification and fluorescence colocalization techniques following near-infrared light irradiation. Moreover, Chechetka et al. confirmed the shape of transformable LM NPs as potential carriers for drug delivery [121]. Using sonication, they first encapsulated carmofur within the EGaIn nanocapsules. A cumulative concentration of 38 mg mL-1 of carmofur was detected after 30 min of NIR laser irradiation, destroying the structures of the EGaIn nanocapsules [121]. The two examples above demonstrate that LM nano-transformers can be used to deliver drugs within cells in a spatiotemporal manner [121]. These results indicate that drug-functionalized LM NPs can be excreted and eliminated by the kidney and bladder systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal doped carbon-dots (M@C-dots) on molten metal NPs
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above in all the sonochemical reactions involving molten metal in addition to the solid precipitate, the supernatant contains very small NPs where the metal is doped in the C-dots the doping level is dependent on the amount of the sonicated metal. In general, the M@C-dots emitted light, but their QY was smaller than the pristine C-dots. In this review we will report on some of the application of the M@C-dots. The physical properties of the M@C-dots were already reported elsewhere [34]. C-dots are consider as quasi-zero-dimensional carbon materials, which have lower than 10 nm diameter in all three dimensions [131]. The first report on C-dots was published in 2004, as a new type of carbon nanomaterials [132]. C-dots are a general term for the many kinds of carbon nanomaterials such as carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, and polymer quantum dots [133]. The major properties of carbon dots are fluorescence, good optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, smaller particle size, water dispersity and simple synthesis methods to the low cost of their synthesis, they are of high priority to researchers [25,134–137]. C-dots contain hydrogen, carbon, and oxygen in substantial quantities on their surface with other functional group [34]. Synthesis of metal doped carbon dots is among the great advantages of the recently developed methodology for sonochemistry of molten metals.

In order to synthesize the M@C-dots, we used sonochemistry of molten metals overplayed by silicon oil and organic liquids, and for each of the four metals (Ga, In, Sn, and Bi), we found that the supernatant contained metal-doped carbon dots. In addition, Kumar et al. have reported a one-step sonochemical synthesis of C-dots doped with a variety of metals (Ag, Au, Ga, Sn, and Zn) that exhibit stable long-term fluorescence. The synthesis of Ag@C-dots and Au@C-dots has been reported for the first time, as a route for synthesizing metals with low melting points (Ga, In, Bi, Sn, Pb, and Zn). This route does not apply to metals with high melting points, such as Ag and Au [127]. Furthermore, the synthesis of Ga@C-dots was described in detail [42,125]. The preparation of Ga@C-dots involves sonicating molten Ga in PEG-400 solution for longer times [138]. The reaction yielded gallium metal doped in carbon dots (Ga@C-dots) [138]. The synthesized Ga@C-dots are obtained in the solution of supernatant and have an average size of ∼6 nm. The fluorescence analysis was used to probe the emission of Ga@C-dots, which was found to differ from pristine C-dots, also in their physical and chemical properties. We attribute the changes of the fluorescence is might be due to energy transfer from the C-dots to the small Ga cluster [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sonochemical-fabricated Ga@C-dots@Ga nanoparticles for biomedical applications
ce:section.ce:para: The abovementioned sonochemical method that left Ga@C-dots in the supernatant finds it was to the solid Ga percipitate as well. It is decorating the surface of the Ga particles with the Ga@C-dots. When the metallic Ga was molten, ultrasonic irradiation was applied, initiating dispersion of small metallic Ga in the form of micro/nano-scale particles of a grey colour suspension. Subsequently, a few small pieces of glass slide (1 cm × 1 cm) were added to the reaction medium which was ultrasonicated for several time periods to make a good coating of Ga@C-dots@Ga NPs on the substrate [139] in a one-step process. The ultrasonication procedure for the self-assembly of Ga@C-dots NPs, deposition on Ga NPs, and subsequent deposition on a surface of glass slide [139]. The structural surface properties of the fabricated Ga@C-dots@Ga nanoparticles and those tightly attached with glass substrate are very closely associated with the various sonochemical parameters [139]. This sonochemically modified glass substrate with Ga@C-dots@Ga nanoparticles was used for further application in neuronal cell growth [139]; the result is explained in section 4.1.

The physical and chemical properties of Ga@C-dots and C-dots were analysed by fluorescence, TEM, SEM, EDS, Raman spectroscopy, EDS, dynamic light scattering (DLS), NMR, and more. Fluorescence of C-dots and Ga@C-dots were measured at several excitation wavelengths (330-490 nm), and the maximum emission was observed between 455 and 550 nm (Fig. 15a and b) [139].

The maximum emission of C-dots/ Ga@C-dots was observed at 460 nm when it was excitation at 390 nm. The Ga@C-dots emission spectra (Fig. 15b) are slightly dissimilar from that of pure C-dots (Fig. 13a) due to the interaction of atomic Ga and C-dots [139]. TEM images were collected from synthesized Ga@C-dots nanoparticles, which showed rather narrow size distribution with diameters of 6 ± 3 nm (Fig. 15c). The large particles of Ga@C-dots on Ga NPs are around 10 nm, and smaller (∼4 nm) NPs are depicted in Fig. 15d. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) micrograph further reveal that Ga@C-dots are highly crystalline in nature (Fig. 15e). C-dots and Ga@C-dots were analysed by HRTEM and EDS; spectra of single particles designate most of the gallium atoms as internal, within the C-dots (Fig. 15e, f). The size of the Ga@C-dots@Ga NPs is 50–200 nm. They were further used for neuron tissue engineering [139], as explained below. Using molten Ga and PEG-400, Kumar et al . have synthesized Ga@C-dots@Ga nanoparticles to regenerate nerve tissue. Several control experiments were conducted to understand which materials of the Ga@C-dots@Ga NPs can affects the neuron cells growth. Initially, neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cell line was implanted onto a C-dots coated glass surface. In a second step, SH-SY5Y cells were also implanted on a Ga@C-dots NPs coated glass surface. Finally, SH-SY5Y cells were implanted on a glass slide that was not coated with NPs. In our publications, we demonstrate that the number of neurites emanating from the soma has been counted. There was a 40% increase in neurites originating from the soma for SH-SY5Y cells implanted on a Ga@C-dots@Ga NPs coated glasssubstrate, compared to cells implanted on the other composites. Furthermore, we found that the Ga@C-dots@Ga coated NPs are more stable and more active than the other three NPs. When SH-SY5Y cell grown on glass coated with Ga@C-dots@Ga NPs, the cells showed 94% increments in the total number of neurites originating from the cell's soma. Cell morphology is significantly affected by Ga@C-dots@Ga. Ga@C-dots@Ga nanoparticles are very significant and interesting to study due to their antibacterial properties along with their morphologic effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sonochemically synthesized Sn@C-dots@Sn anode for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs)
ce:section.ce:para: In the last two decades, the research on developing new batteries has grown dramatically. The rapid growth in the human population is accompanied by increased consumption of energy. Conventional sources such as petroleum and natural gas are predicted to be depleted in the foreseeable future, therefore demand for energy storage and generation is rising steadily around the globes [140–142]. Capacitors and batteries are traditional energy storage devices, which can be replaced by rechargeable batteries. These devices offer good safety, energy density, and performance characteristics.

In the beginning, graphite was used as an anode, but recent investigations of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon nanotubes, nitrogen-doped graphene sheets, and doped carbon nanotubes have shown promising results [143]. In order to improve the electrochemical performance of rechargeable batteries, it is possible to tune the physicochemical properties of carbon dots doped with various materials [144,145]. Kumar et al. synthesized Sn@C-dot@Sn NPs (Sn NPs was decorated with Sn doped C-dots) by a facile sonochemical synthesis route involving molten tin. Fig. 16 presents cyclic voltammetry results (Fig. 16a), charge and discharge profiles (Fig. 16b) and a rate capability study followed by constant rate cycling (Fig. 16c)[33]. Please explain what the results are is it better than other anodes nothing is said on the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molten metal doped carbon quantum dots (supernatant)
ce:section.ce:para: As an efficient sonochemistry of molten have generated two products: 1) solid precipitate, which contain metallic nanoparticles and (2) supernatant contained metal doped carbon dots. This novel synthesis route has improved the intrinsic properties of metal doped carbon dots (M@C-dots) and metal ions-based C-dots recently receive good attention. Therefore, the summary about the accomplishments of M@C-dots doping with molten metal atoms has been continued for their applications in antibacterial, biological, and energy-related areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ga@C-dots for the eradication of Pseudomonas aeruginosa
ce:section.ce:para: The number of bacteria resistant to available antibiotics has steadily increased over the years. Due to the limited availability of antibiotics, the development of novel antibiotics has been in demand. Material science and nanotechnology are two promising alternatives. Nanoscale materials have demonstrated enhanced antibacterial properties [146]. Numerous studies have been conducted on the antibacterial activity of nanomaterials such as metals (silver, iron, gallium) [125,147–150], carbon-based materials, and metal oxides (ZnO, TiO2)[151] Among carbon-based materials, C-dots offer many advantages, including their small particle size, fluorescence properties, high cellular uptake, water solubility, biocompatibility, and biodegradability. Recent studies have demonstrated that carbon dots doped with Ga NPs and fabricated sonochemically have interesting antibacterial properties against P. aeruginosa [125]. In this case, it can be attributed to the combined antibacterial properties of both C-dots and atomic gallium. A sonochemical prepared Ga doped C-dots showed excellent stability over two months, and antibacterial studies revealed MIC values of 0.34 to 1.36 ppm for gallium doping in the range of 70 to 340 g per gram of C-dots [125]. According to these results, Ga@C-dots could potentially be used as antimicrobial agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ga@C-dots ointment for low drug resistance against Leishmaniasis
ce:section.ce:para: Leishmaniasis is an infectious disease transmitted by sand flies that is caused by obligate intracellular protozoa from the genus Leishmania [152]. Sonochemically synthesized C-dots offer excellent biocompatibility, superior optical properties, and a low cost of preparation, which make them a suitable choice for the treatment of skin disorders. This additional application of Ga@C-dots synthesized sonochemically has been explored by Kumar et al. The authors investigated the application of Ga-doped C-dots in formulating an ointment containing fluorescent C-dots. The authors were the first to report an optically active CD-based ointment, without side effects and high solubility, for the treatment of leishmania donovani [124].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low melting point metal doped C-dots as lubricants
ce:section.ce:para: Only a some of the reserach works have been reported on the published on tribological properties of doped C-dots, especially Ga doped C-dots (Ga@C-dots) and nitrogen doped C-dots (N@C-dots) and comparing their tribological behaviours with those of pure C-dots. Kumar et al. synthesis of C-dots and gallium doped C-dots (Ga@C-dots) using sonochemistry, PEG-400, metallic Ga. Prepared C-dots showed excellent tribological performance as potential lubricants. Doping further enhanced anti-wear performance C-dots [153]. However, the C-dots, N@C-dots, and Ga@C-dots have unsatisfactory anti-wear AW action relative to the reference oil [153]. A study suggests that doping C-dots with various elements can enhance their anti-wear and extreme-pressure properties [153].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Novel Sn@C-dots-modified TiO2 catalyst as a photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic properties of TiO2 (titanium dioxide) has already been demonstrated and corelated with several other materials. C-dots and functionalized with different metal and metal oxide can be used as catalyst for the selective hydrogenation of various halonitrobenzenes [154]. Xu et al. reported the carrier of N-C-dots for broaden the photoresponse range visible light region, accelerate charge transportation and separation for the photocatalytic decomposition of Rhodamine B (RhB)[155]. Metal decorated C-dots with TiO2 nanosheet are utilized by Yu et al. in the photocatalytic performance and was measured by degradation function of rhodamine-B under solar light [156]. The authors of this study also investigated the use of sonochemically synthesized C-dots in photocatalysis and polymerization reactions [157,158]. C-dots nanocomposites also demonstrated excellent catalytic performance [159]. In a two-step sonochemical procedure, Kumar et al. formulated the Sn@C-dots/TiO2 nanocomposites. A molten tin and PEG solution were sonicated at 260 °C in order to form Sn@C-dots. Added TiO2 to the solution resulted in further sonication, facilitating Sn@C-dot adhesion to TiO2. Photocatalytic performance of the fabricated nanocomposite was evaluated under solar illumination. The photodegradation of methylene blue and crystal violet under solar light was more efficient than that of TiO2 and C-dot/TiO2[159]. Under sunlight, Fig. 17 illustrates how Sn@C-dot/TiO2 as a photocatalyst causes photocatalysis, charge separation, and photodegradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion and future perspective
ce:section.ce:para: In this review, we summarise the synthesis of molten metals and their composites NPs with other metals, polymers, or organic molecules via sonochemical synthesis method. The effect of different experimental parameters on the NPs products, e.g., on their shape and size we also focused. First, it should be noted that sonochemistry is the only general method for synthesis of molten metal particles without any surfactant or catalyst. This method was applied to all low melting point metals (<420 °C), namely to Hg, Ga, In, Bi, Sn, Pb, Cd, Sb and Zn metallic system. It is resolved that compared to other synthesis methods, the sonochemically synthesized molten metal-based nanoparticles, alloys/intermetallic compounds, and metal-doped C-dots are less toxic and constitute excellent materials for catalysis, chiral imprinting, biomass to biofuel, and biomedical applications owing to their good biocompatibility with living system. The sonochemical techniques can be designed molten metal micro/nanoparticles in a single step without the use of a catalyst, surfactant, and any other additive. Optimization of sonication parameters such as frequency/amplitude of probe sonicator, tip morphology, sonication power, and time sonication time is needed to prepare the different kinds of hybrid molten metal particles with desired properties. Molten metal-based particles are promising materials in a range of applications such as catalysis, antimicrobial agents, chiral separation, and neuron tissue engineering owing to their excellent physical, chemical, optical, mechanical, and electrical properties.

Furthermore, the authors would like to thank the Department of Chemistry and Bar-Ilan Institute of Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA) at Bar-Ilan University for providing the infrastructure and continuous support for this research.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Vijay Bhooshan Kumar holds a Master of Technology with distinction from the University of Hyderabad, an Innovative Master of Engineering thesis award from the Indian National Academy of Engineering, and an Outstanding PhD from Bar-Ilan University in Chemistry/Nanotechnology (Excellent Graduate Student by the Israel Chemical Society) in 2017. He then joined the Los Alamos National Laboratory in the United States as a Director's Postdoctoral Fellow (2018-2020). Currently, he is a Postdoctoral researcher at Tel Aviv University, Israel. His research interests include nanotechnology, molten metal, nanoscale metal, sonochemistry, fuel cell, peptide-based biomaterials, DNA nanotechnology, nano-chem-bio-interfaces in cancer and biomedicine.
